data.title: SPYI Vs. XDTE: The Crash Showed Us These Funds' True Colors
data.description:
Introduction

I've been fairly bullish on the future of derivatives ETFs, especially in the last few years, as more actively managed covered call strategies have hit the market and changed the game as we know it.

Two of my favorites, for different reasons, have been the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (BATS:SPYI) and Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (BATS:XDTE).

I've covered both funds in the past on their own, and given buy ratings to both funds. Most recently, I covered XDTE as a way to "weather the storm," which I posted around the market bottom in April. My last SPYI exclusive coverage was back in December, but I've compared it to other funds more recently, like JEPI back in late March.

At the time when I wrote the "weather the storm" line in April, I had no idea that we would bounce back so quickly, spurred on by a turn-about in the tariff policy that brought the markets down in the first place.

Very recently, I learned that this flip-flop behavior and its whipsaw affects on the market is being called the "TACO trade."

How Did They Handle the Big Swings?

The question for both SPYI and XDTE as S&P 500 covered call ETFs isn't how they would handle the downturn, but how they would handle the recovery. It's common for covered call funds to outperform the index, even if just slightly, on the way down, since the income from the covered call component pads the downside (again, just slightly). From February 19th, the last market top, to April 8th at the market bottom, we saw this in action.

Both funds incur essentially all the downside of the S&P 500, but the income they generate is distributed in cash, so it helps buoy the return of the funds. While it's nicer to drawdown 16% instead of 18%, it's still not very nice.

Keep this in mind when investing in these kinds of funds.

What should concern investors is not the downside, since that is a known equation (you lose basically what the index loses), but how much upside these funds participate in on the way back up if and when that happens.

The nature of covered call funds means that their upside is capped, and if you incur all the downside, but little of the upside, you fall behind quickly.

One of the oldest covered call funds, QYLD, which I've called "old and out of style," falls into this trap, where its upside has never been enough to make up for the downside it incurs. Subsequently, over its lifetime, it has lagged its index something fierce.

Year-to-date, we can see that while its reference index, the Nasdaq 100, recovered from those April lows and is now back toward being even for the year, QYLD never recovered all the way. Its upside was capped, and once that cap was breached by the market, QYLD stopped gaining much in value.

So, what about SPYI and XDTE? Thankfully, we have QYLD's sister fund, XYLD to compare to on this front, to see the "worst case" for a covered call fund.

Please don't attack me in the comments for that last use of air quotes, XYLD investors, but please do take a long look at this next chart.

Here are the same funds, SPYI and XDTE, plus XYLD, from where the first chart ends — right at the very bottom.

This is a consequence of the capped gains of each fund, and the style of options management they use. XYLD is a passive index that rolls at a certain point in time every month. See when XYLD hits its upper cap?

Over time, moments like this happen, and the differences in strategy can make or break a given fund. That 10% gain XYLD investors missed out on from the market rebound isn't made back easily. SPYI investors are surely happy their miss was less than 3%.

If you invest in covered call funds, these kinds of misses are impossible to avoid. It just comes with the territory. The goal is to do it as little as possible.

What's the Difference?

The key difference between these funds is their options execution strategies.

Strategy wise, both have the same foundation:

  1. Buy the S&P 500.
    • SPYI owns long stock just like an S&P 500 index fund.
    • XDTE owns long call options on SPX, which are settled in cash, and serve to provide the same exposure as long stock.
  2. Sell call options against that position for income, using short call options on SPX, which are settled in cash, avoiding early assignment risk and providing tax efficiency.
    • SPYI sell out-of-the-money ("OTM") calls, leaving upside for the whole month.
    • XDTE sells at-the-money ("ATM") calls, leaving little upside for the day, but resets every day, and does not cap its gains overnight.

The second part is why their results differ during different market conditions. While both funds use the same short positions, SPX call options, they implement them in different ways.

SPYI operates an actively managed strategy aimed at rolling options every month, although management is able to intervene and manage the options on the fly, as-needed. This has helped them navigate sudden market shifts, such as the April crash and rebound.

XDTE also operates an actively managed strategy, but does not have the onus to "intervene" between call option sales. This is because, instead of selling them monthly and adjusting sooner if needed, XDTE sells options every day, that expire the same day they are sold ("0DTE"). Management has to sell a new call each day, meaning they have to evaluate and re-position every day.

The daily reset feature of XDTE was one of the ways in which the fund is supposed to outperform other covered call funds because its upper cap is re-adjusted at market open each day, and it receives all the overnight gains/losses to the index, which historically is when most of the largest positive moves in the index happen.

For reference, XDTE's expense ratio ("ER") is 0.97% and SPYI's is 0.68%, but all performance charts shown in this article are net fees.

SPYI Has a Little Trick Up Its Sleeve

So, it sounds like XDTE would be the better fund to consider, since it is not always short (long overnight), re-adjusts constantly, and is more capital efficient than SPYI since it obtains long exposure from call options instead of stock positions.

However, SPYI has one major advantage that we saw in action in April, and is the reason that it has outperformed over XDTE in its lifetime (XDTE is the younger fund, and so the furthest back we can take the comparison is its inception).

W can see that XDTE has kept up with the market, even outperforming, during the 2024 bull run. In a bull market scenario, XDTE is a clear winner.

But not all markets are bull markets.

In the market's recovery phase in April, SPYI showed its hand and excelled, overtaking XDTE's performance. From SPYI's website:

The Fund utilizes a call option strategy that may include both sold and purchased SPX index options, which may provide the opportunity for upside capture in rising equity markets.

I said earlier that SPYI utilizes an index fund-like construction method, where it owns long stock. The fund also, tactically, owns long call options in order to offset its upside cap, providing more upside to the fund. This means they are able to manage the short option less, and when its cap is hit, or likely to hit in their view, they are able to "charge up" the fund with long call options to increase their upside participation.

All charts up to this point have been "total return," which includes dividends paid, but not reinvested. Here are the prices of the funds by themselves,

Of course, both of these funds introduce manager risk, or key-person risk, where investors are reliant on management to make active decisions day-to-day.

There does come a risk with this strategy, as "charging up" your upside with long call options may mean more downside if the managers are wrong. Timing is key, and while it seems that SPYI nailed the timing this time around, that doesn't speak for the future.

Being reliant on the timing and skill of the managers to make up for deficiencies in the covered call strategy is a risk many investors may not want to take, especially if they consider that the long call options strategy SPYI employs hasn't mattered all too much until recently, during this flash crash.

Before then, XDTE was outperforming. Here is a chart of XDTE's inception to the peak in February of this year, just the bull scenario.

XDTE was outperforming SPX itself, which was a massive feat, and one that rightfully garnered a lot of attention from investors. We know from pulling this chart out to the full lifetime (shown earlier) shows XDTE as the worst performing asset of these three. Performance charts are all about timing.

Here is the takeaway:

  • If you expect a choppy or down market, SPYI is the better choice.
  • If you expect a secular bull market, XDTE is the better choice.

SPYI is unlikely to keep up with the index or with XDTE in pure bull markets because the managers don't tend to employ the long call options regularly, only in circumstances where they feel like the market is about to, or already has, breached their upside cap. They don't hold these options on a typical market day. They are the little trick in the sleeve, and the less they have to employ them, the better.

XDTE is unlikely to keep up with the index or with SPYI in choppy or down markets, as its uncapped overnight nature and daily-reset behavior works against it, capping upside on days when the market is violently moving upwards after recovering from flash crashes.

SPYI vs. XDTE Dividends & Yield

One of the major differences between these funds is their level of distribution. Since XDTE writes ATM options, its payouts are larger (and more frequent) than SPYI. It pays weekly, and carries a distribution rate of nearly 32%.

In contrast, SPYI pays monthly, and its distribution rate is a little over 12%.

We can see how these contribute differently to total return, as SPYI's gap between price and total return is far more narrow than XDTE's, which ends up distributing more, but also making consistent price losses. This latter behavior is not necessarily a bad thing, and occurs in a significant number of derivative-based funds, but investors should note it alongside their risk tolerance. In any case, this is why I tend to prefer only look at total return when comparing these funds.

For reference, XDTE's expense ratio ("ER") is 0.97% and SPYI's is 0.68%, but all performance charts are shown net fees.

The preference between monthly and weekly is all semantics to me, as the end result quarter-by-quarter is the same. If investors prefer to have the weekly payouts, XDTE is a clear winner, but if it doesn't matter, SPYI seems to offer a more stable payout, bar the dip in April due to the buying of call options and rolling of the short call, but as we saw earlier, that move helped SPYI outperform during that timeframe on price returns even if its dividend was lower. There is some give-and-take, but it is generally a fairly even-payer, whereas XDTE has far more variable and volatile payments.

Regarding taxes, this has been a topic of significant discussion as of late. Both of these funds distribute the often-preferable return-of-capital ("RoC") distribution rate. For more on that, check out this article I recently published on SPYI's sister fund, QQQI, which employs the exact same strategy but with the Nasdaq 100 instead of the S&P 500. In that article, I discuss the tax situation in depth.

The way that QQQI's distributions for taxes are handled applies to both SPYI and XDTE, as it is a by-product of their use of short SPX call options.

Conclusion

Ultimately, investor preference between the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (BATS:SPYI) and Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (BATS:XDTE) comes down to a few factors:

  • Their view of the market conditions:
    • SPYI for a sideways or down market.
    • XDTE for a secular bull market.
  • Their preference for income vs. returns:
    • SPYI typically has less price loss, but a lower yield, at 12%.
    • XDTE typically has consistent price loss, but a higher yield, at 32%.
  • Their preference for income timing:
    • SPYI pays monthly.
    • XDTE pay weekly.

For the market conditions, I am still a believer that the choppiness we've seen so far this year isn't over, especially since we are seeing the market still moved by political headlines day-to-day, which tend to overreact and overcorrect too often. This makes SPYI a better pick from my view, though both funds retain their buy rating from me.

I have a preference for total return, not income or price gains. So on that front, I would lean toward SPYI. If an investor needs the increased income, and is willing to give up more upside in rebounding markets to get it, they would prefer XDTE.

Personally, that last part doesn't matter to me, but that is fairly personal. I know from the comments on my past articles that some of my readers greatly appreciate the flexibility of the weekly payouts. On this front, frequent is usually better, so I'll give XDTE a small win here.

Neither fund is a poor choice for income investors looking to benefit from equity premium exposure through options, and both funds are part of a new-breed of options-based income funds that have impressed me, and so both keep their buy ratings.

However, if an investor is locked between the two choices, I vote SPYI.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: West Pharmaceutical Services: Still Potential Downside Ahead
data.description:
Today, I am putting West Pharmaceutical Services (NYSE:WST) in the spotlight. The stock dropped sharply in mid-February but seems to be finding a bottom in recent weeks. As an article in 2024 on Seeking Alpha noted, West Pharmaceuticals Services provides the 'Picks & Shovels' to the injectable drug industry. The stock is down some 35% so far in 2025. Is the dip in the shares buyable? An analysis follows below.

West Pharmaceutical Services is headquartered in Exton, PA. The company's primary focus is developing, making and distributing containment and delivery systems for injectable drugs and healthcare products both in the United States and globally. The stock currently trades just over $210.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of just over $15 billion.

The company has two primary business segments: Proprietary Products and Contract-Manufactured Products. The first business division produces items like stoppers and seals for injectable packaging systems; syringe and cartridge components as well as myriad products for injectable solutions. Contract-Manufactured Products manufactures and assembles devices and components used in surgical, diagnostic, ophthalmic, injectable, and other drug delivery systems. These include GLP-1 auto-injectors as well as other self-injection devices. The company is the global leader in the manufacturing of the rubber components that seal injections called elastomers. It should be noted that the gross margin from Proprietary Products is more than twice that of Contract-Manufactured Products and that Proprietary Products now make up approximately 80% of overall sales.

The big fall in the stock followed Q4 results posted in mid-February. West Pharmaceutical Services beat both top and bottom-line expectations but posted initial FY2025 sales and earnings guidance that were significantly under the consensus at the time.

Recent Results:

West Pharmaceutical Services posted its Q1 numbers on April 22nd. The company delivered non-GAAP earnings of $1.45 a share, just over 20 cents a share above the consensus. This was down from $1.56 a share in the same period a year ago. Profit margins rose slightly to 33.2% from 33.1%.

Revenues rose just .4% on a year-over-year basis to $698 million. That still beat tepid expectations by just over $10 million. Net sales from the Proprietary Product division grew by 0.6% to $563.0 million. Organic net sales growth within this business segment was 2.4%. Gross margins improved to 37.3% from 37% in 1Q2024. Contract-Manufactured Product sales fell .8% from the same period a year ago to $135 million. Organic sales growth came in at .8%. Biologics and Pharma sectors saw mid-single digit growth while Generic sales were off mid-single digits. Gross margins fell to 16.1% from 17% in the same period a year ago.

Leadership also boosted its FY2025 guidance slightly. Management now sees net sales between $2.945 billion to $2.975 billion, up from its previous guidance of $2.875 billion to $2.905 billion. Non-GAAP EPS guidance rose slightly to $6.15 to $6.35 a share, from $6.00 to $6.20 a share previously.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

The analyst firm commentary is largely constructive around the outlook for West Pharmaceuticals. Since first quarter results hit the wires, five analyst firms including Jefferies and Citigroup have reissued Buy ratings on the stock. Price targets proffered range from $245 to $300 a share. TipRanks maintained its Hold rating and $228 a share price target.

Just over three percent of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. There has been no insider activity in the shares since October of last year. West Pharmaceutical Services ended Q1 with just over $400 million worth of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet against just over $200 million of long-term debt according to the 10-K filed for the quarter. West Pharmaceutical Services spent $133.5 million during the quarter to buy back its own stock, at an average price of just over $242.00 a share. The company spent $560.9 million in FY2024 to buy back stock at an average price of $354.30 a share.

Free cash flow for Q1 was $58.1 million. This was up sharply from $27.6 million from 1Q2024, but a large part of that was due to the decline in capital expenditures. At that current run rate of Q1, WST would have a free cash flow yield of 1.5%. In FY2024, the company delivered free cash flow of $276.4 million. This would give WST a free cash flow yield of 1.8% on that data point.

Conclusion:

West Pharmaceuticals Services had $6.75 a share (non-GAAP) on $2.89 billion in revenues in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has profits falling to $6.30 a share even as sales rise slightly to $2.96 billion in FY2025. They see earnings rebounding to $7.16 a share on five to seven percent growth in FY2026.

That leaves the stock trading at just over 31 times trailing earnings and just north of 5.2 times revenues. The shares pay a tiny dividend yield of .4%. That seems a quite extreme given profits are expected to fall this fiscal year and grow EPS at just over a 2% CAGR from 2024-2026. The stock is also selling at premium to the S&P 500, which trades around 23 times earnings and has much higher earnings and revenue growth projected for 2025 and 2026.

In addition, over half of the company's sales come from overseas. Management stated their current projected net tariff impact in the final three quarters of 2025 will be between $20 million to $25 million. Finally, the company has spent a huge amount of its cash flow in the last five quarters buying back its stock at much higher prices than current trading levels. Hardly the best use of cash in retrospect given the company still has some long-term debt on its balance sheet. Therefore, I am passing on any investment recommendation around WST despite the recent pullback in the stock. The shares still seem an Avoid even with the stock 35% lower than when it began the year.

data.title: How Trump Might Bypass U.S. Courts To Continue His Tariff Agenda
data.description:
By Inga Fechner, Carsten Brzeski, Chris Turner

The ruling

On May 28, a US federal court, the Court of International Trade, delivered a landmark ruling that US President Donald Trump overstepped his authority by imposing tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The court found that the IEEPA does not grant the president power to unilaterally impose broad import duties, especially when the declared national emergencies - such as drug trafficking, immigration, and trade imbalances - are not directly addressed by the tariffs themselves.

The court’s ruling mainly follows the argument that a trade deficit that has persisted for 49 years does not meet the requirement of an “unusual and extraordinary threat”.

The tariffs affected by the ruling

  • Fentanyl and immigration-related tariffs on imports from China, Canada, and Mexico, ranging from 10% to 30%.
  • Global trade surplus tariffs of 10% or more, including reciprocal tariffs that were suspended until July 9.

The tariffs not affected by the ruling

The decision does not impact tariffs imposed under other legal authorities, such as:

  • Steel and aluminium tariffs (25%), in force since March 12.
  • Autos and auto parts tariffs (25%), in force since April 3 and May 3, respectively.

What happens now

The court has given the White House 10 days to halt the affected tariffs. However, the administration has already filed an appeal to the US Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit. The case is widely expected to reach the Supreme Court, which could ultimately decide the legality of the IEEPA-based tariffs.

If the ruling is upheld, the US government may be required to refund tariffs collected under IEEPA, which amount to some $13.7bn up to April 30, according to US Customs and Border Protection. A significant portion - $7.9bn - was paid on goods from China and Hong Kong.

What this means for future trade policy

Regardless of the outcome, the ruling is likely to shift the focus toward tariffs imposed under other trade laws and acts, such as:

  • Section 232 (national security)
  • Section 301 (unfair trade practices)

These statutes require more extensive investigations but ultimately still allow the president to act unilaterally. Several ongoing investigations could lead to new tariffs on:

  • Pharmaceuticals
  • Semiconductors
  • Trucks
  • Critical minerals
  • Seafood
  • Cranes
  • Copper
  • Lumber
  • Aircraft
  • Shipbuilding

Pending investigations/Section 232 and Section 301 tariffs to come

(Source: White House, Federal Register, ING Research)

Additionally, the administration retains the option to use Section 122 (balance of payments) and Section 338 (retaliatory tariffs), further expanding its tariff toolkit, as we’ve discussed previously here.

The bottom line

This ruling marks a significant legal and political challenge to the use of emergency powers in trade policy and adds another layer of uncertainty to the whole US trade policy. It also signals that companies and countries will likely intensify efforts to eliminate sector-based tariffs, which have become a favoured instrument of US trade enforcement. As of April 30, these tariffs had generated $26.8bn in customs revenue for FY2025.

We expect the pace of sector-based tariffs to accelerate, with additional measures likely to be announced soon. This will be important for ongoing negotiations with the EU and China; the negotiations that have the strongest impact on the global economy and financial markets. In this regard, we warn against too much relief, or even complacency, at this point.

In the case of Europe, the largest part of tariffs affects the automotive sector, and this part simply remains in place. If the US administration moves along with the intended tariffs or quotas on European pharmaceuticals, the largest part of European exports to the US would still be subject to tariffs of more than 20%.

Finally, the pushback against President Trump could trigger more aggressive tariffs or other economic policy announcements, which may impact key trading partners. Let’s not forget that one justification behind current trade tensions was not only the US trade balance deficit but also the search for additional government revenues. The lack of which would further fuel the current debt (un-)sustainability debate.

Market reaction: A relief rally

US equity market futures greeted the court ruling with a rally of just over 1%. This reaction was no doubt helped by some encouraging first-quarter results from Nvidia (NVDA). US Treasuries sold off 5bp across the curve on what could be some better news for the US consumer. However, we note that the 10-year US swap spread continues to trade wide at 55bp, and there could be some concerns that a lower tariff revenue take will only compound fiscal concerns as President Trump pushes his expansionary bill through Congress.

The dollar has enjoyed a modest bounce on the news that Washington’s tariff policies do face some constitutional oversight after all. A large risk premium has been embedded in the dollar since April. This ruling could alleviate a little of that risk premium and allow the dollar to reconnect with some dollar-positive interest rate differentials. However, a significant dollar advance looks unlikely given the threat of new sectoral tariffs and concerns in the US Treasury market this summer.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Kinder Morgan: Upcoming Cash Taxes Will Hit Distributable Cash Flow Progress
data.description:
Introduction

Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI) is a leading energy infrastructure company in the US with 79,000 miles of pipelines and 139 terminals. Its midstream services are crucial for moving fossil products, especially natural gas, all over the US.

In this article will I touch on the change in investor perception that made KMI go up over 50% including dividends in the last 12 months after mostly flat-lining for years before that.

I will then discuss the current growth outlook for Kinder Morgan and also why, on a cash basis, it will be much less impressive than it seems at first. This is due to an upcoming tax hit that is not very much discussed yet.

Finally, I will look at the current valuation and the risks of investing in Kinder Morgan before giving my final verdict on it.

In that, I am trying to answer the different questions different people have. Those that own the stock, might ask themselves if KMI has gotten ahead of themselves and if it is time to trim now or even sell out completely. Those not in the stock might ask themselves if they missed the boat after 50%+ gains. And those poor kindred souls, that bought into broken promises over a decade ago, will surely ask themselves if now is a unique opportunity to get out with their self-esteem finally restored, or if they might be missing the ride of the century by exiting now. I hope, the following discussion helps everyone with their decision as I look at what is in store over the next few years.

Returning to growth

Kinder Morgan has come back from the dead with a vengeance, outperforming even the S&P 500:

The last couple of years, Kinder Morgan management spent their time trying to convince investors that they won't go out of business anytime soon due to the renewable energy boom. In their 2023 investor day presentation they had slides like this:

They also spent billions in acquisitions and capex to position themselves for a less carbon intensive future. They even created a new division: RNG, short for renewable natural gas.

However, for all their efforts and investments, KMI could not really show much growth. For the last decade, EBITDA was fairly stagnant with only minor growth:

And as growth evaporated so has the premium the shares traded at (plus they cut the dividend which they declared safe just before they cut it).

But as mentioned before, shares have recently outperformed even the tech-heavy S&P 500. Why is that?

Firstly, they hit an inflection point in that the issues, that hunted them in the past, were left behind. They had too much debt and therefore had to divest assets. In addition, they had many contracts roll over and renew at lower rates. All of this countered the additional EBITDA from their continued growth capex spending leading to only mediocre growth. With these headwinds now mostly past, their future capex could finally start driving growth. Growth capex backlog was at $3bn at the start of 2024. Then this happened:

In the last 1.5 years, their project backlog nearly tripled to $8.8bn, way above their historic norm. Not only would their capex finally show up as growth as they left their headwinds behind, but also their opportunity set has massively increased. KMI suddenly seemed to enter growth mode again. Rich Kinder confirmed this during KMI's Q3-2024 call:

In fact, in my decades of experience in the mid-term arena, I've never seen a macro environment so rich with opportunities for incremental build-out of natural gas infrastructure. And at Kinder Morgan, we expect to be a major player in developing that infrastructure.

How did that happen?

Natural gas demand was now expected to increase sizably over the next couple of years. As utilization rates of existing transportation systems had crept up over the last years, new capacity was now necessary. This high demand was driven by mostly two factors:

LNG feedgas is expected to more than double. This is due to, firstly, Europe moving away from cheap Russian gas after Russia's invasion into Ukraine. And secondly, by Europe and Asia needing natural gas for their transition towards greener energy by idling coal-fired power plants or building new gas fired plants as backup for a renewable dominated future. While the forecasted increase in demand only translates in 3-4% annual growth, most of the LNG-driven growth should happen in Texas and Louisiana, where KMI is especially well-placed to take advantage (see map in the introduction).

As one could see above, KMI forecasts an even higher demand growth than Wood Mackenzie because KMI is much more positive on demand from utilities driving natural gas demand. They see demand for such things as coal to gas conversion, increased manufacturing on-shoring (should tariffs work as intended), renewable energy backup and, especially helpful as a narrative, much higher power demand due to AI usage and the needed data center.

Rich kinder commented during the Q1-2024 conference call as follows:

One recent survey showed a projected increase in electric demand to power data centers of 13% to 15% compounded annually through 2030. Put another way, data centers used about 2.5% of U.S. electricity in 2022 and are projected to use about 20% by 2030. AI demand alone is projected at about 15% of demand in 2030. If just 40% of that AI demand is served by natural gas, that would result in incremental demand of 7 to 10 Bcf a day.

All of this taken together shifted investors' perception of KMI. Not surprisingly, especially the AI-driven power demand captivated investor's imagination. Goldman Sachs helped by forecasting a massive increase in data center power demand from AI:

However, for all the big numbers like a 165% increase in power demand for data center, this only leads to a much less impressive increase in overall electricity growth in the US to a 2.4% CAGR for the rest of the decade. KMI mentioned during their Q2-24 call that some projections get as high as 4.7% vs. historically 0.5%.

Now, perception certainly has changed for KMI, but how much does it really mean in terms of cash earnings?

Quantifying KMI's EBITDA growth outlook

KMI's growth over the next few years will be driven by two factors. Firstly, by the underlying earnings growth of their existing assets and secondly, by their growth capex and acquisitions.

Weirdly enough, we get less information about the first than the second. They have long-term contracts with built-in escalators. But unlike REITs that often show like-for-like rental growth or how much upcoming re-letting levels differ from current rents, we only get very limited insight into what to expect here. It normally boils down to one slide during the annual investor event, like here in 2023:

As mentioned above, Kinder Morgan had several years during which they were hit by lower recontracting rates, holding back EBITDA progression. 2023 they told investors that this would be the last year with meaningful recontracting risk to the tune of $0.2bn. Otherwise, they expected a $0.3bn improvement in EBITDA just from the base business. That would mean for 2024 onward we should expect to see the whole $0.3bn or roughly 4%, right? Well, not exactly, as 2024 and 2025 investor presentations show:

As one can see, the additional EBITDA is $0.1bn for 2024 and even $0.0bn for 2025. Therefore, much worse than one might have expected after the 2023 slide. To make matters worse, one does not really know if $0.1bn in 2024 means $149m rounded downwards or only $51m rounded up.

$100m on $7.6bn EBITDA means roughly a 1.3% growth rate. I believe, somewhere between 1% and 2% seems like a reasonable assumption for long-term underlying growth given the numbers KMI provides. Let's not forget that not all their businesses benefit from escalators. One might also realize, that KMI missed their budgeted EBITDA forecasts for both 2023 and 2024. To be fair, they managed to beat it nicely in 2022.

What is also obvious from above slides, is, that growth capex really drives growth at KMI even before their project backlog tripled. They commented on their future expansion capex spending during the Q4-2024 conference call:

some estimate of some additional growth beyond what's in the backlog because as someone noted, the backlog adds up to $8.1 billion and if you take four years of $2.5 billion, you get $10 billion. So, there is a little bit of capital that we're assuming based on our opportunity set, we'll be able to fill in.

Basically, right now, they guide towards investing $2.5bn in growth capex annually for the next four years plus maybe some acquisitions:

Yes. So, we think about M&A on a very opportunistic basis. And so we can't predict that. And therefore, it's hard to budget or schedule for it.

How will that translate into additional EBITDA? here is a slide from their most up-to-date investor presentation (May 2025):

They guide towards an EBITDA build multiple of 5.9x. This means that for every $1bn in capex they expect to generate about $170m in EBITDA (normally year 2). This would mean that over the next 4 years they would generate an additional $1.7bn in EBITDA based on a targeted $10bn growth capex. That would be a 21% increase over the 2024 adj. EBITDA or roughly a 5% annual growth rate over four years excluding any acquisitions.

Together with the underlying growth rate of 1-2% we get to an overall EBITDA growth of 6-7%. By start 2029 we should then have over $10bn in adjusted EBITDA run-rate, up from $7.9bn in 2024. Of course, KMI could add sizably to its backlog over the next three years, and acquisitions could add to that. On the other hand, their $10bn capex guidance already includes some additional growth capex.

These are the numbers based on current management guidance. In the valuation section further down I will try and assess what risks these projections carry and whether some discount is warranted.

On one hand, EBITDA is useful as it focuses on a business operational profitability. On the other hand, some people like to call EBITDA earnings before bad stuff. Because in the end, what counts, is how much cash a business produces for its owners. Kinder Morgan management certainly agrees on that perspective. They have long been stating, that distributable cash flow is their preferred measure for value creation and state as much on their website:

We focus on cash flow instead of net income because we believe cash is the best gauge of underlying asset performance. Kinder Morgan’s Distributable Cash Flow (DCF) has historically been an accurate proxy for sustainable cash flow. The main difference between net income and DCF is net income is reduced by GAAP depreciation and DCF is reduced by sustaining capital.

How will cash flow develop over the next few years? Will there be operational leverage? As we will see in KMI's case the "bad stuff", especially taxes, hits cash flow fairly hard.

Higher interest costs are manageable

One of the items that might slow cash earnings progression is higher interest payments. Kinder Morgan has a huge debt load of well over $30bn. However, the biggest refinancing risks are by now behind them.

Most of their debt now has coupons not too far away from current market rates, with the exceptions shown above. In addition to above, they have a 375m for 4.15% in 2026 and another $1.25bn due in 2028 with 4.3%. But other than that, the rest of their debt mostly yields somewhere between 5-8%.

KMI just placed some debt for 5.15% for 5 years and 5.85% for ten years. 30 year debt trades at around 6.5%. For simplicity, I assume a 6% rate for refinancing their upcoming debt. Overall this gives me $95m or roughly $100m in increased interest costs from rolling over low coupon debt over the next few years.

Kinder Morgan intends to finance their growth capex mostly from internally generated cash flows. However, they are also willing to use their balance sheet capacity to take up new debt if necessary.

Operating cash flow was between $5bn and $6.5bn in the last three years. Let's take about $5.7bn as a base for internal financing power. Yes, this will increase as growth capex comes online. But as we will see later on, there are also tax forces in the opposite direction.

Besides growth capex, KMI of course, also needs to fund sustaining capex for their existing businesses of roughly $1.2bn annually. Taking this altogether, KMI needs $2.6bn for its dividend, $2.5bn for growth capex and $1.2bn for sustaining capex or a total of $6.3bn vs. only about $5.7bn in operating cash flow to fund it. This means, even absent any acquisitions, they might need to take on an additional $2-2.5bn in debt over the next four years. This would lead to additional interest costs of about $135m.

Thus, refinancing and higher debt levels could reduce cash flow by about $230m over the next four years.

KMI becomes a cash taxpayer

Kinder Morgan has not been paying taxes for so long that investors can be forgiven to ignoring the subject altogether. They talked about this in 2019, but since then, many investors forgot about it as an issue.

This positively impacted operating cash flow:

As we can see above, their operating cash flow benefited from not paying cash taxes to the tune of around $650-700m annually. While they show a statutory tax rate in their in come statement, they actually do not really pay those taxes in cash. This is mostly due to prior losses. However, they will soon run out of operating tax losses:

This in turn will lead to their cash tax rate rising into the low double digits from basically non-existent (well, maybe 1-2%) as they forecast in a slide in their most recent business update presentation:

As they mention Corporate Alternative Minimum Tax (CAMT) themselves, it is likely they will be subject to that, once they run out of operating losses which are allowed under CAMT. By 2027 they should be paying at least 10%-percentage points more in cash taxes than 2025 as per their slide. Unfortunately, the slide stops at 2027.

General Business Credits are a very general term. They might consist of energy credits relating to their RNG or CCS business.In 2024, they related to investment tax credits in their biogas business:

Whether those will be available under the new administration remains to be seen. Which is probably what they refer to as "future policy changes", besides it being a boilerplate statement.

My best guess for the years after 2027 is that their effective cash tax rate should be somewhere between 15% and 21%, 21% being the statutory tax rate. Even though they start paying a bit of tax in 2026, I assume that they can still make use of some tax credits in 2027 as they state that their use of GBCs is limited. Thus, I believe there is still some carry-over from 2026 to 2027 and the ultimate cash tax rate is likely to be higher than what they showed for 2027 and thus most likely higher than 15% (which is also the CAMT, see above). The upper band should be the 21% statutory tax rate. I might, of course, be wrong there, but this will be my working assumption going forward.

In 2024, they had a total income before tax of $3.4bn but paid nearly no cash taxes. In the years before, it was even less. This means that their cash flow should be lowered by $500-700m annually, depending on what effective cash tax rate they end up, plus any future income generated by their extensive capex program will also be subject to much higher tax rates.

Obviously, this will have both an impact on valuation and on the actual cash earnings progression over the next few years.

How much additional taxes will they have to pay on their $1.7bn EBITDA? EBITDA gets reduced by depreciation and interest payments to end up with taxable income. Interest we assumed $135m and depreciation was roughly 6% of the asset base in 2024, which would be $600m on $10bn investment. This would lead to taxable income of $965. Assuming a 17% effective cash tax rate would get us to $165m in taxes. Overall, interest and taxes reduces EBITDA by $300m from $1.7bn to $1.4bn in net cash earnings.

But KMI will also have to pay additional cash taxes of $500-700m p.a. and face the $100m additional interest from rolling over debt. Therefore, the effective increase in operating cash flow from the big investments over the next four years will only be $600-800m. Add to that maybe $400m organic growth for a total improvement of $1-1.2bn. That will only be a 19% increase in OCF over four years, or an annual growth rate of only 4.4%.

Distributing cash flow improvement will be slightly better as one would expect that initial sustaining capex should be low for newly built projects, and it is overall a lower number to start from. There, the increased cash flow of $1-1.2bn would lead to a roughly 5% CAGR. Without the tax effect, it would have been a much better 8%.

While the tax hit will only come from 2027 onward, the same is unfortunately also true for KMI's cash flow from their growth capex. Their biggest projects in their backlog a very back-end loaded.

As we can see, DCF growth will stay relatively moderate at around 5% p.a. for the next four years, despite a huge increase in KMI's backlog. While KMI finally left headwinds from delivering and contract renewals behind, they unfortunately now face a new one with increased cash tax payments. This cuts the expected growth rate over the next years nearly in half and pushes investor's rewards for KMI's continuous capex spending once again further out

Valuation

What does it mean for valuation? I fully agree with KMI that cash flow is the way to value them. More recently, KMI has shifted the focus of their presentations more to EBITDA progression than DCF and I guess we can see why above. I will stick with cash flow to owners.

Valuation via Multiples

The most basic idea of valuation is looking at dividend yield and its sustainable growth rate to get an idea of what my expected return looks like. KMI currently yields around 4.2%. As we saw above, for the next few years, underlying cash flow growth will only be around 4.4%. As KMI needs a lot of their internally created cash flow for growth capex, I would expect actual dividend growth to be below that number. The most recent 1.7% increase sounds like a good guideline over the next few years as KMI focuses on growth. Longer term, however, the tax headwind will be behind KMI, and they eventually should be able to reap the benefits of their continuous growth investments. I would not expect it to be as high as 7% but maybe 5-6% if they continue to invest, occasionally do an acquisition and partially fund it with debt. This means over the longer term, one might expect a 9-10% return here. But over the next few years dividend growth should be muted.

Another approach would be to calculate the DCF yield. KMI guides towards $2.32 in DCF per share for 2025. But as we saw above this is without paying tax. Assuming they would already pay cash taxes in 2025 would get us to a DCF/share of about $2.07. Based on the current price this gives us a 7.4% DCF yield (instead of 8.2% without taxes). Now, as DCF assumes solely sustaining capex and no growth capex, we can only add the organic growth of the existing businesses. As we saw above, those are very low. maybe 1-2% per annum. That would give us an 8.4%-9.4% expected return on a DCF yield basis. Assuming future growth projects will create value above the cost of capital would indicate that this is the lower band of expected returns.

Therefore, valuation multiples and the respective inverted yields guide to an expected return at the current price of about 9-10% which indicates neither over- nor undervaluation.

Valuation via a discounted cash flow model

Finally, I also modeled a DCF. A DCF is better able to capture the multiperiod movements of a complex company.

The main assumptions are a 10% discount rate, which is fairly standard. Furthermore, I assume a cash tax rate of 16% which is between the 15% minimum tax and the 21% statutory tax. KMI has always been very good at reducing their tax bill. But as we saw above and also from their own projections, they will run out of losses to offset their gains. As such, their tax will increase substantially from 2027 onward, but I assume it will not fully reach the 21% statutory tax rate.

Furthermore, I assume organic growth of 1.5% as discussed above. The values KMI provides for the last years were between 0% and 4%.

For sustaining capex of 2.5% of the asset base and depreciation tax shield of 6.2% of the asset base, I use KMI's historic numbers. That said, sustaining capex is always a matter of contention because one could argue endlessly to what extent e.g. a new, more powerful compressor is sustaining or growth capex. Long-term growth capex of 5% of the asset base is in line with their $2-3bn historic experience. I assume a 7x EBITDA multiple, slowly declining towards 8x.

As we can see clearly in 2026 and 2027 the much higher tax bill slows cash earnings progression. In 2028, I arrive at my normalized tax rate and from thereon it does no longer negatively impact growth rates. But, of course, taxes still reduce available cash flow to shareholders.

My model gives me a fair value of $34 or over 20% upside.

The best thing about a DCF is, that it allows you to see the sensitivities to the underlying assumptions. In this case, the DCF is extremely sensitive to the organic growth assumption. At 1% the fair value drops to $30.36 at 2% it increases to $37.41. These are big swings for minor changes of 0.5%-points.

Important is also how profitable growth capex will be. I am a bit more conservative compared to management guidance. That is because historically, my impression was that KMI tends to be overly optimistic or, at least, that it does not really show up in the numbers. I also wonder to what extent KMI might be pushing for high initial rates while accepting lower escalators as an offset. But if I were to give them full credit with a 5.9x multiple, that would increase my fair value to $38.17. If we face more cost overruns, more competition and more delays in growth capex, bringing the assumed EBITDA multiple to 8x from the start, the fair value drops to $31.53.

It is yet unclear where KMI will eventually end up in terms of federal cash tax rate. I currently assume 16% as KMI has always been smart in reducing cash taxes. However, should KMI have to pay the full statutory tax rate of 21% this would lead to a drop of my fair value of $31.55.

One can also back solve the expected return at the current price. On my base assumptions, a discount rate of 11.25% solves to the current share price. This is higher than what we see with the multiples. That should not come as a surprise, as my DCF continues to include profitable growth capex for a long time period after the initial tax hit. On the other hand, the DCF also shows how sensitive the valuation is to several inputs like the underlying growth rate on a sustaining basis. Unfortunately, there is a lack of good guidance, which reduces the reliability of the DCF outcome. As such, even a decent upside like the 20% should be taken with a grain of salt.

Risks:

Renewable energy won't go away. There is a reason why KMI spent so much time in the past talking about renewable energy. The current administration clearly favors oil & gas over renewables, but generally speaking, renewables are here to stay. They are also likely to continue to gain market share over the long term, even without government subsidies. In addition, storage solutions constantly get better and cheaper. There is long-term risk to natural gas demand, at least from the power side. Certainly, nothing to worry about for the next few years, but eventually some of KMI's assets might end up stranded in the worst case. And once KMI enters a state of decline, debt will matter again. It is a major risk but many years out.

Interest rates could stay high or even go higher. Moody's just downgraded the US and rates moved up. KMI constantly needs to refinance debt. Higher rates mean higher interest payments going forward. While they have by now mostly refinanced their very low yielding debt, an overall higher interest rate level is still a negative for such a highly levered entity as KMI. As they also intend to occasionally tap into debt markets to finance their high capex plans, this is doubly true.

While KMI is mostly insulated against price swings thanks to a large proportion of fixed take-or-pay contracts, they are not totally immune to lower commodity prices. As such, a recession with lower demand and lower commodity prices and volumes could still hurt them. Plus, should those persist for a longer time, that might also hurt their upstream customers.

Tariffs might increase the cost of steel etc. and this might impact KMI's capex. They remarked that for 2025 they feel well-prepared, but there is some risk going forward of higher costs of capex due to tariffs.

From an operations perspective, we're still in the process of evaluating the impact of tariffs. We don't expect it to be material to 2025, but the uncertainty of tariffs along with commodity prices did cause us to be a little bit more conservative in communicating our outlook for the year at this point.

That might lower expected returns from projects while increasing the need to finance capex with additional debt.

Kinder Morgan mentioned several times high competition for projects they bid on. This seemed to have contributed to the higher EBITDA-multiples compared to the past. Continued increasing competition could therefore lead to lower returns from growth capex.

Final Verdict

Based on the different valuation approaches, I get to an expected return range of 9-11%. Overall, this seems fair given, the uncertainties surrounding the company.

Uncertainties include the long-term future of natural gas, potential for cost overruns at an ambitious growth capex program, as well as my DCF being highly dependent on several key assumptions into which we have little insight, especially what we should expect as organic growth rate. This lack of transparency warrants some discount in my opinion. I wish KMI would hold an investor day focused on that topic. On the flip side, they might continue to increase their backlog at a high pace and surprise to the upside. They also started doing acquisitions again, like with Outrigger. Therefore, I might be too conservative.

Without the tax issue, I would have given KMI a buy, as my DCF would have shown a fair value north of $40. But the hit to valuation from becoming a cash taxpayer brings it back into neutral territory. Personally, I am holding on to my position here.

Summary:

KMI had a horrible time from 2015 to early 2024. They overpromised, were overlevered and contract roll-overs constantly lowered their base income. But by the end of 2023 it seems they have finally worked through these issues. Luckily for them, the demand environment of their business has changed to the positive as well. They should be growing nicely over the next few years. But then, this outlook seems mostly reflected in the strong run-up the stock had over the last year.

Unfortunately, their growth in cash earnings will be slowed by them having to pay cash taxes again. This reduces cash returns by 15-20%, depending on where they end up with regards to cash taxes. Unless, of course, they figure out a way to reduce taxes going forward. But this is nothing I want to build an investment case on.

Overall, I feel comfortable holding a key piece of energy infrastructure in the US, which is well positioned to benefit from growing demand for natural gas. At the current price, one can probably expect decent, fairly save 9-11% returns from KMI. This warrants a hold rating to me. It is borderline to a buy rating if I were to give full credit to management forecasts. But I admittedly lack the confidence to do so. KMI always talked about their backlog being highly profitable, but somehow it never showed. In addition, I feel there is a lack of transparency and information when it comes to key inputs, like for example, the underlying EBITDA growth rate on a purely sustaining basis. Therefore, I am so far reluctant to give them full credit for their forecasts.

If KMI dropped back to the lower $20s, I would probably get more constructive. On the other hand, if KMI run up to mid-30s, I might start to trim. As of now, I simply hold.

If you found this article helpful, please vote for it as a compelling analysis. If you have questions or remarks, please comment below. I would love to hear from you. Many thanks!

data.title: John Hancock Global Shareholder Yield Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The MSCI World Index, the fund's benchmark, suffered a modest loss in the first quarter.
  • The John Hancock Global Shareholder Yield Fund (MUTF:JGYAX) delivered a positive return and strongly outperformed the benchmark.
  • Dividend-paying stocks outpaced the broader market, creating favorable conditions for the fund due to the nature of our strategy.

Market review and outlook

Uncertainty regarding U.S. tariffs weighed on broad-based global equity indexes in the quarter. Most of the downturn was the result of weakness in the United States, where stocks were hurt by concerns that Washington's trade policies would cause a resurgence in inflation and dampen slower economic growth. The decline in investor sentiment, while a headwind for the benchmark, was primarily confined to U.S. information technology stocks and other higher-growth companies. On the other hand, value stocks, international equities, and dividend payers all outperformed as investors rotated into market segments that had been out of favor. This shift proved to be a significant positive for the fund, particularly when stocks weakened in the second half of the period.

In terms of our outlook, we see inflationary pressure, demand erosion, economic disruptions, and continued market volatility as being potential effects of the recently announced tariffs and the possibility of prolonged trade tensions. The ultimate impact on the real economy and corporate fundamentals is largely dependent on the duration and magnitude of tariffs. The U.S. Federal Reserve is in easing mode, but the path for monetary policy has been clouded by growth pressures and upside risks to inflation stemming from tariffs.

We're cautiously optimistic on the outlook for the economy and equities from a fundamental standpoint, but risk management is essential given the uncertain trade climate and wide array of possible outcomes. In this vein, we believe the fund is poised to capture the productivity of a growing economy while remaining defensively positioned for resilience if growth begins to falter. We maintained a diversified portfolio of high-quality companies with reliable free cash flow growth and longstanding track records of sound capital allocation practices. It's impossible to predict how or when the tariff war will be resolved, but we maintain conviction that our portfolio companies have the ability to adapt to a challenging time and even emerge stronger on the other side. We believe diversification across regions, sectors, and business models provides an additional cushion against uncertainty and volatility.

Contributors and detractors

Stock selection was the primary contributor to relative performance in the quarter. The fund's holdings exceeded or finished in line with the return of the corresponding benchmark components in ten of eleven sectors, with materials being the sole exception. We produced the widest margin of outperformance in communication services, healthcare, and consumer staples. The top contributors at the individual company level generally reflected the defensive nature of the market, with stocks such as Phillip Morris International, Inc. (PM), AbbVie, Inc. (ABBV), and International Business Machines Corp. (IBM) leading the way. A position in CVS Health Corp. (CVS), which rebounded from last year's depressed levels as investors regained confidence in its business, was an additional contributor of note. On the other hand, the five largest detractors were information technology stocks that gave back ground following robust performance in 2024. Among these were three stocks that were hurt by waning enthusiasm about the growth potential of AI: Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Co. (HPE), and Dell Technologies, Inc. (DELL)

Portfolio changes

As is typically the case, our portfolio activity was fairly light in the quarter. We initiated positions in Microchip Technology, Inc. (MCHP) and the Norwegian energy company Equinor ASA (EQNR), and we sold the fund's position in the Canadian telecommunications provider BCE, Inc. (BCE)

data.title: Aurora Innovation: Slow Ramp To Actual Success
data.description:
Aurora Innovation, Inc. (NASDAQ:AUR) achieved a huge accomplishment in the last month, but the company is a long way from actually success. The autonomous trucking company still has a long path to real revenues and a ton of money to raise in the process. My investment thesis is still Bearish on the stock, even after the 35% dip from the last Sell rating.

Slow Ramp Ahead

Just at the end of April, Aurora Innovation officially launched the Aurora Driver autonomous trucking service via the Houston to Dallas lane in Texas. The company started with just one truck and has recently expanded to two trucks operating daily during daylight while avoiding inclement weather.

The whole promise of the service is that going from a few trucks to thousands of trucks is just a matter of time and scaling up. Unfortunately though, Aurora Innovation doesn't plan to scale beyond 10 trucks by the end of the year.

In addition, the Houston to Dallas lane doesn't capture the real benefits of the autonomous trucking service. The real benefit is routes where hours of service limitations restrict drivers from driving more than 11 hours, allowing the Aurora Driver to utilize trucks at a higher rate and make faster deliveries.

Aurora Innovation is working on opening up the key Ft. Worth to El Paso route, followed by the extension to Phoenix. The projection is this 1,000+ mile autonomous lane can be delivered in half the time using the Aurora Driver without service limitations.

Of the 50 billion VMTs on the table for 2028, over 42% of the trips are over 1,000 miles where an autonomous driving service offers considerable benefits. Only 17% of trips are under 300 miles to where a driver can make a round trip without running into service limits requiring spending the night on the road and idling the truck.

Long Path Ahead

An investor can quickly look at the huge jump in revenues forecast for the years ahead and start making a case for paying the $11 billion valuation for the stock. The issue is that the revenues aren't guaranteed and the current goal is only $331 million in 2027.

A couple of examples in the AV space highlight the need for caution. First, Tesla (TSLA) has promised robotaxis for a decade now and the EV company is only promising a launch in June while Waymo, owned by Google (GOOG), already has operations in multiple cities. In this case, the expected AV leader is now lagging the developments in the sector.

The second issue is Cruise Automation, which probably fits more with the risk in Aurora Innovation. The AV company owned by General Motors (GM) had launched robotaxi services in California and an accident not even caused by Cruise led to the company pausing service and never re-starting.

Aurora Innovation targets spending ~$180 million in cash per quarter for the rest of the year while revenues won't ramp towards material amounts until 2027. For this reason, the CFO is guiding towards needing ~$750 million in additional funds to reach cash flow positive in 2028.

On the Q1'25 earnings call, CFO David Maday reiterated the long path ahead to reaching cash flow positive:

In addition to our core growth initiatives, growing the Aurora driver powered fleet, expanding the lanes we operate and unlocking customer endpoints as well as introducing lower cost hardware, we will continue to evaluate opportunities to further advance our competitive position and or de-risk certain elements of our business. Taking these factors into account, we now expect to raise $650 million to $850 million prior to achieving positive free cash flow, which anticipated in 2028.

Aurora Innovation currently has a market cap of nearly $11 billion, so the funds won't be overly dilutive. The biggest issue is having to raise funds at lower stock levels, if the business were to struggle with ramping operations, or ran into a disruptive accident.

Another potential issue is the business model. The company plans to charge by mile for the Aurora Driver services. The Driver-as-a-Service (DAAS) won't be cheap with the company predicting only potentially saving somewhere around 25% of the drivers cost, which amount to an average of $0.97 per mile.

Back on the Q1'25 earnings call, CFO David Maday talked about pricing more around the costs of a human driver:

So we think we’ll deliver tremendous value and that will provide us potential to price somewhat equivalent to a human driven comparison. But even with a slight discount to that, I think we’re going to have really robust margins. And I think there is an opportunity to transform the industry, make it safer and also create tremendous value for everybody and kind of that’s our focus.

Aurora Innovation predicts costs saving to come from fuel efficiency and lower insurance costs. Customers might expect more direct costs savings with fuel efficiency benefits not guaranteed and future competition could attempt to under price the service.

For these reasons, an investor should be skeptical of paying 12x 2028 sales targets with too much potential for error.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Aurora Innovation remains the one autonomous based company priced for success, though the business is only not starting to generate revenues. The company still has a long path to actually being a successful business and investors should wait for a better value to buy the stock.

data.title: Transocean: The Picture Is Getting Clearer, But It's Not Clear Enough
data.description:
Back in February of this year, one company that I decided to reaffirm as a ‘hold’ candidate was offshore drilling business Transocean (NYSE:RIG). At the time, the company was seeing some positive indicators that suggested that future results might improve. On top of this, shares of the company looked quite cheap, particularly on an absolute basis. Even so, I was discouraged by the declining backlog of the business. That meant to me that things had not yet bottomed out. Fast forward to today, and the stock is trading 17% lower than it was back then. That compares to the 0.9% drop experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time.

Even though the company is getting tantalizingly cheap, backlog continues to shrink. Management seems optimistic about the long-term outlook for this industry. And under normal circumstances, I would probably claim that now would be a good time to upgrade the stock to a ‘buy’. Unfortunately, the decline in energy prices and broader economic concerns caused by the global trade wars that erupted because of the US create a lot of uncertainty regarding what is certainly a high cost of production for this segment of the energy industry. Because of this, I am not yet ready to upgrade the business. But if shares fall further from here, or if we start to see some additional clarity in the energy industry, an upgrade could be in store before too long.

A look at recent results

Since I last wrote about Transocean, investors have had data come out about one additional operating quarter. This would be the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the business was $906 million. That's 18.7% above the $763 million the business reported one year earlier. This growth can be attributed to a couple of factors. For starters, the number of operating days for the company’s rigs grew from 1,785 to 1,940, pushing the rig utilization rate for the enterprise up from 53.7% to 63.4%.

This indicates growing demand for the company's services. However, this has materialized in another form as well. During the quarter, the average daily revenue for one of the company’s rigs hit $443,600. That's the highest it has hit in several years and represents a meaningful increase over the $408,200 reported one year earlier. The company also benefited to the tune of $30 million from increased activity associated with its newbuild ultra-deepwater floater Deepwater Aquila. All combined, this translates to a positive environment for the offshore drilling industry.

Even though revenue for the company improved, the business went from a net profit of $98 million to a net loss of $79 million. Even on an adjusted basis, it worsened from a net loss of $22 million to a net loss of $65 million. The big contributor to this weakening was the fact that, in the first quarter of 2024, the company enjoyed an income tax benefit of $191 million. That compares to a $39 million expense incurred this year. If we look at before tax profits, the company actually improved significantly from a loss of $93 million to a loss of $40 million. Other profitability metrics ended up coming in mixed. For instance, operating cash flow went from negative $86 million to positive $26 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $144 million to $99 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business improved from $199 million to $244 million.

We don't really know what to expect when it comes to 2025 in its entirety. Management did say that revenue should come in at between $3.85 billion and $3.95 billion. At the midpoint, that would represent a 10.7% rise over the $3.52 billion the business generated last year. In all likelihood, profits and cash flows will rise as well. But because this is a pretty volatile space anyways, it would be unwise to project out much from there. Instead, if we use results from 2023 and 2024, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. These are multiples that I consider to be appealing. While the price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple is particularly low, in light of the high amount of debt the business has, $5.96 billion on a net basis, I think it would be better for investors to give more weight to the EV to EBITDA multiple.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Transocean 2.8 7.0
Seadrill Limited (SDRL) 48.7 3.2
Patterson-UTI Energy (PTEN) 2.2 18.4
Noble Corporation (NE) 4.5 4.8
Helmerich & Payne (HP) 2.7 4.2
Valaris Limited (VAL) 5.4 4.5

In the table above, I then decided to compare Transocean to five similar companies. It seems to be more or less in the middle of the group. I say this because, on a price to operating cash flow basis, two of the five competitors are trading at levels that are lower than it is. But when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, four of the five are trading lower. Considering that we are giving more weight to the latter, one could even argue that the stock is perhaps a bit pricey compared to the competition.

Long term, management seems optimistic. For instance, they expect global deepwater investment to rise by 10% this year. And from 2026 to 2027, they expected to grow another 16%. And by 2030, they expect annual deepwater investment to be 40% higher than it is today. Some of the near term success that the company is banking on includes the expectation that rig demand in Brazil generated by Petrobras (PBR) is expected to grow to over 30 active rigs by the end of this year and should grow even further next year. It just so happens that Petrobras is the company's second largest customer, accounting for 21% of total revenue last year. So its success should be the success of Transocean as well.

It might seem unlikely that demand for offshore drilling could grow so significantly over the next few years. However, management ties this to a couple of factors. For starters, between 2023 and 2030, total global oil production is expected to climb by 13% from 100 million barrels per day to 113 million barrels per day. But the actual amount of oil that will need to be extracted is far greater than a 13 million barrel per day rise. This is because depletion is expected to take 43 million barrels per day off of the space, which means that total new production of 56 million barrels per day will be needed. Management also claims that at prices of $50 per barrel or greater, the offshore industry is viable. By comparison, for the prolific Permian Basin, we are looking at between $62 per barrel and $64 per barrel, depending on whether we are talking about the Midland Basin or the Delaware Basin.

As great as this inheritance is, there are certain challenges that the company is going to face. For starters, backlog continues to fall. At the end of the first quarter, backlog was $7.93 billion. That was down from the $8.33 billion reported at the end of last year and it compares to the $8.86 billion reported one year ago. Seeing as how backlog is a leading indicator of future revenue, it is disconcerting that this metric has not at least bottomed out as of yet. Once it does, I will feel a lot more optimistic.

Another issue relates to the overall net debt of the business. It has dropped nicely from $6.67 billion in the first quarter of 2023 to $5.94 billion in the final quarter of last year. It did tick up modestly, however, to $5.96 billion in the first quarter of 2025. Obviously, we can't expect every quarter to experience improvements in an industry that is known for volatile cash flow figures. But I would still like to see management bring overall net debt down as quickly as possible. Unfortunately, because the market capitalization of the firm is $2.13 billion, it can't really do much with the net debt in terms of issuing equity. Its only options are to use excess cash flows and/or proceeds from asset sales. As of the end of the first quarter of 2025, the company had $344 million in assets held for sale in the form of two of its ultra deepwater floaters. Those were originally under contract to be sold, but that contract has now been terminated because the buyer has not been able or willing to make payment.

Another issue at hand here is the supply and demand environment for oil. Because OPEC+ is now raising production in order to try to bring some of its members to heel when it comes to their commitments to cut output, and because the global trade war that the US started under the Trump Administration, oil prices have been on the decline. From the time I last wrote about Transocean earlier this year until today, Brent crude prices have dropped from $74.76 per barrel to $64.81 per barrel. This still means that offshore drilling is profitable according to management. However, if the economy is heading in the direction that I think it is, I wouldn't be surprised to see further weakening in this space in the near term. That would certainly put a damper on investment decisions for companies that have, in recent years, been hesitant to make big commitments on projects that will take a while and tremendous amounts of capital to get up and running. The fact that interest rates remain elevated because of inflationary concerns caused by the tariff situation also makes it more expensive for companies to operate. So that's a negative as well.

Takeaway

It does appear to me as though the future, if we focus on the long haul, is looking better for Transocean. However, there are certain near-term concerns that investors should be aware of. The company is showing some nice growth on the top line and some of its bottom-line figures are improving. However, net debt has ticked up recently and backlog continues to shrink. In light of this mixed picture, I believe that maintaining a ‘hold’ rating on the company is the right choice at the moment.

Despite tariff uncertainty, China started 2025 with a very strong quarter. Gains were fuelled largely by DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) announcement, which prompted a wave of AI-driven optimism and increased investor confidence in China’s ability to innovate beyond tech sanctions. This triggered a sharp rally in the shares of Alibaba, given that it has the largest cloud business in the country and is therefore viewed as a key beneficiary of these AI-related developments.

Tencent, which has the second-largest cloud business in China, was another standout performer. PICC Property and Casualty (OTCPK:PPCCF) also fared well after posting a positive set of full-year results. However, not holding Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) and BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), which are seen by the market as indirect AI beneficiaries and beneficiaries of government policy, proved costly. Xiaomi is a direct beneficiary of the consumption trade-in programme put forward by the government. Additionally, our mainland-listed A-share companies trailed as both onshore and offshore investors rushed to invest in AI beneficiaries listed offshore in Hong Kong. As a result, we underperformed in China by about 140 bps.

Semiconductor and tech hardware stocks came under pressure following the DeepSeek announcement as investors speculated that more efficient generative AI models might lead to lower demand and capex in the tech value chain and, ultimately, lower growth. Revisions in capex and earnings from some US companies have crystalised concerns. Consequently, our Taiwanese holdings in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (TSM), Accton Technology, Delta Electronics, Yageo Corp and MediaTek (OTCPK:MDTTF) detracted and were responsible for around half of the underperformance during the quarter, though not holding Hon Hai Precision Industry mitigated the losses.

Outside Asia, the off-benchmark position in Latin America-based IT services company Globant (GLOB) was another main drag on performance. The company’s fourth-quarter results were in line with expectations, but the official 2025 guidance indicated a softening of expected demand in its core market, the US. In South Africa, gold miners rallied strongly as gold continued to touch new highs. Our lack of exposure to a number of these names was negative.

On the flip side, stock selection in India was robust. Core holdings such as Power Grid Corporation of India and Indian Hotels rose sharply in March as the central bank’s liquidity boosting measures and a well-received budget from the government seemed to lift sentiment.

Investments in the United Arab Emirates, Abu Dhabi Islamic Bank and Aldar Properties, contributed further to relative returns.

Market review

Emerging market equities outpaced developed markets in the first quarter as the consensus around US exceptionalism began to unwind. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index rose by 3.01% in US dollar terms. Political developments in the wake of US President Donald Trump’s inauguration in January dominated sentiment, particularly his tariff plans and the global fallout. The US Federal Reserve paused its interest rate cuts amid increased economic uncertainty. Concerns that tariffs could derail US growth also weighed on the US dollar, which boosted some markets.

Among major markets, China led returns despite escalating tariffs from the US. Chinese start-up DeepSeek’s model that was trained without the most advanced chips. Separately, policymakers committed to more fiscal stimulus and set a 2025 growth target of around 5% during the annual parliamentary gathering.

Indian equities were dragged down by the slowing economy for most of the quarter. In March, the central bank’s measures to add liquidity and improving economic indicators began to revive investor confidence.

Elsewhere, Gulf bourses closed mixed on concerns over oil prices. Latin America was buoyed by gains in Brazil and Mexico. Brazilian stocks rebounded amid sentiment that the market was oversold, while Mexico has thus far effectively navigated the tariff conflict with the US.

Activity

We added three holdings in China. Commercial lender China Merchants Bank conducts business domestically and internationally. Li Auto (LI) designs, develops and sells new energy vehicles. Shenzhen Envicool Technology is a provider of temperature control systems. We also bought India-based J.B. Chemicals and Pharmaceuticals, which makes pharmaceuticals, chemicals and agro-based products.

Conversely, we sold Credicorp (BAP), Ping An Insurance (OTCPK:PNGAY), Saudi Arabian Oil Co and Sungrow.

Outlook

Consensus heading into 2025 was one of US exceptionalism with tax cuts, deregulation and tech dominance leading US and global equities higher. However, with valuations as stretched as they had been heading into the year, there was scope for a downside surprise. The emergence of Chinese AI start-up DeepSeek, combined with a tariff-inclined Trump, appears to have provided the downside surprise.

However, this environment still offers reasons for optimism for emerging market investors.

At the time of writing, Trump has announced his tariffs. They set a 10% baseline for all countries with more punitive reciprocal tariffs for identified nations, though most of the higher tariffs have now been pushed back, except for China, which has hit back at the US with levies of its own. Overall, the tariffs answer some key questions and suggest that Trump’s goals of shifting manufacturing and raising revenue for tax cuts are prime and centre.

Tariffs on China were stacked, but we believe the key driver for Chinese equities will continue to be the domestic environment, where the outlook remains more positive, with further support likely in the coming months. India is well-positioned to navigate a more fractured global economy, backed by long-term structural drivers and IT service exports, which are not currently under fire.

Emerging market valuations are still attractive, both historically and compared to the US. Our portfolio companies are delivering results, which we believe the market will reward. We remain confident in our holdings and their ability to navigate market crosswinds.
